---
title: "Florida Pellitory (Parietaria floridana)"
slug: "parietaria-floridana"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180792417/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Florida Pellitory"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Rosales"
tax_family: "Urticaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Rosales", "Urticaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180792417/medium.jpg" alt="Florida Pellitory" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/lucieb30">
lucieb30</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107488192">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Rosales
-   **Family:** Urticaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=166124)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common in Florida found in shady, moist, disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A low-growing herb characterized by its delicate, translucent stems and small, inconspicuous greenish flowers. In Florida, it is commonly found in shaded, moist habitats such as hammocks, rock outcrops, and along walls or tree bases. It grows throughout much of the year in warm conditions and often forms loose patches in sheltered areas.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/166124-Parietaria-floridana)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2328)
